---
title: "Cloud Boundary — Case Study"
subtitle: "Governing GCC Moderate compliance gaps with UIAO"
doc-type: case-study-pattern
audience: [Customer, Sponsor, Program Lead, Authorizing Official]
classification: Public
boundary: GCC-Moderate
status: framing-pattern
canon-source: "docs/docs/gcc-boundary-solution-architecture.qmd"
canon-reference:
  - "docs/docs/gcc-boundary-problem-statement.qmd"
  - "docs/customer-documents/architecture-series/boundary-impact-model.qmd"
  - "docs/customer-documents/compliance/boundary-authorization/B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd"
derived-from: "uiao canon"
---

# Cloud Boundary — Case Study

::: {.callout-important}
## Framing pattern, not a fabricated engagement
This page is the **canonical engagement pattern** for a GCC-Moderate
boundary case study. Specific agency identifiers and metrics are
intentionally absent until real engagement material is reviewed.
:::

[IMAGE-01: GCC-Moderate boundary engagement pattern diagram. Center shows the GCC-Moderate authorization boundary as a vertical line. Left of the line: M365 Commercial reference (with full feature availability). Right of the line: M365 GCC-Moderate (with five named features in dimmed/red boxes — Endpoint Analytics, Device Health Attestation, Locations & Network Fence, ARC Server Monitoring, Expedited Updates). Below the boundary line, a horizontal "UIAO compensation pattern" band shows four mechanisms: Boundary Probe, In-Boundary Telemetry, Compensating Enforcement, ATO Documentation Surface. To the right, an outcome panel labeled "Boundary impacts as first-class governed objects". Clean engineering blueprint style, dark navy (#0D1B2E) and teal (#1E8C8C) on white background, with red (#C74040) reserved for unavailable-feature highlights. No photographs, purely diagrammatic.]

## Engagement archetype

A federal agency operating M365 in GCC-Moderate discovers — typically
late in an ATO cycle or during ZT analytics rollout — that a cluster
of features advertised in admin portals and referenced in the SSP do
not function as documented. The pattern matches the
[GCC-Moderate Boundary Problem Statement](../../docs/gcc-boundary-problem-statement.qmd):
features appear available, are described in commercial documentation,
and are referenced in migration plans, but silently produce no data,
emit no errors, and have no documented resolution path.

The agency's challenge is **structural**, not configuration: the
authorization boundary itself attenuates outbound telemetry that
drives many of the platform's higher-order analytics, and Microsoft
does not clearly document which features behave this way.

## Starting state (typical)

- M365 GCC-Moderate tenant in production.
- SSP and migration plans reference features whose live behavior is
  inconsistent with documentation.
- ZT analytics, anomaly detection, and insider-risk programs
  partially blocked by silent telemetry gaps.
- Compliance posture nominally compliant; substrate observability
  structurally below commercial peers.

## UIAO intervention pattern

The substrate addresses each impact dimension via in-boundary
mechanisms (per
[`gcc-boundary-solution-architecture.qmd`](../../docs/gcc-boundary-solution-architecture.qmd)):

### 1. Boundary probe

A programmatic test of which features actually function on each side
of the boundary, producing structured capability disposition data
rather than relying on portal screenshots.

### 2. In-boundary telemetry

Adapters operating inside GCC-Moderate emit canonical claims with
explicit `source_classification: derived` for signals substituting
for unavailable commercial features. The provenance envelope makes
the substitution explicit; downstream consumers can see what is
authoritative vs. derived.

### 3. Compensating enforcement

Agency-side enforcement adapters for controls that platform features
cannot deliver in-boundary. Their evidence flows through the same
canonical evidence chain.

### 4. ATO documentation surface

Boundary impact is captured as a **first-class artifact** that the
SSP, POA&M, and Component Definition all reference. Specific feature
gaps become named, modeled, and drift-monitored objects rather than
implicit footnotes.

## Substrate behaviors observed (pattern)

A real engagement would document:

- **Capability disposition matrix** — for each cited M365 feature in
  the SSP, the actual in-boundary disposition (functional / partial /
  silent / blocked).
- **Telemetry-fidelity gap closure** — what percent of unavailable
  signals are now substituted via in-boundary adapters with derived
  classification.
- **Compensating-enforcement coverage** — which ZT controls are
  agency-enforced via UIAO-anchored adapters where platform features
  are unavailable.
- **ATO package change** — net SSP/POA&M edit volume to bring the
  package into alignment with substrate truth.
- **3PAO re-execution outcome** — does the assessor's independent
  rerun produce a byte-identical bundle?

## Why this is different from "remediating findings"

Two distinctions matter:

1. **The substrate names the boundary as a first-class object.** A
   conventional remediation cycle treats each missing feature as an
   isolated gap. The substrate treats the boundary itself as the
   governed object, with the four impact dimensions as its
   structured properties.
2. **Compensating mechanisms become canonical, not bespoke.** Each
   compensating adapter is registered, schema-validated, drift-
   monitored, and OSCAL-emitted. The pattern survives staff
   transitions and vendor changes.

## Lessons that propagate back into canon

A real engagement typically yields:

- New entries in the boundary capability disposition data
  (`src/uiao/canon/data/gcc-moderate-telemetry-gaps.yaml`).
- Refinements to the boundary impact model where new dimensions
  emerge (rare, but possible — would require an ADR).
- Compensating-enforcement adapter additions to
  `modernization-registry.yaml`.

## Promotion to "real engagement"

Same posture as the identity case study: requires reviewed,
externally releasable material; canon-change traceability for cited
lessons; sponsor-side review and approval.

## Related documents

- [Architecture Series — Boundary Impact Model](../architecture-series/boundary-impact-model.qmd)
- [GCC-Moderate Problem Statement (canon)](../../docs/gcc-boundary-problem-statement.qmd)
- [GCC-Moderate Solution Architecture (canon)](../../docs/gcc-boundary-solution-architecture.qmd)
- [B.1 GCC-Moderate Boundary Model](../compliance/boundary-authorization/B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd)
